title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a boy called Leo Martin, who plays chess in competitions.
audio_script: Interviewer: Leo Martin is sixteen and plays chess in national competitions. Leo, how did you first learn the game? || Guest: My grandfather taught me when I was seven. At first I only played because it was something we did together on Sunday afternoons. [20]I became serious about it after beating an older student at a school club, which made me think I could improve[/20]. || Interviewer: What was difficult about playing in your first competitions? || Guest: I expected to feel nervous during the games, and I did, but that wasn't the main problem. [21]I found it hard to sit still for such a long time and stay focused until the end[/21]. Some games lasted three hours. || Interviewer: How do you prepare now? || Guest: I study famous games and practise online, but I don't spend all night doing it. [22]My coach says physical exercise helps concentration, so I run three times a week before school[/22]. It sounds strange, but it works. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy about chess? || Guest: Winning is nice, obviously, but it isn't everything. [23]I like the moment when a position suddenly makes sense and I see a plan that wasn't clear before[/23]. That feels exciting. || Interviewer: Do your friends understand your hobby? || Guest: Some do. Others think chess is boring or only for quiet people. [24]They're surprised when I tell them competitions can feel as tense as any sports match[/24], especially when time is running out. || Interviewer: What are your ambitions? || Guest: I'd love to play internationally one day, but I'm realistic. [25]For now, I want to help run a chess club for younger children at my school[/25], because learning early helped me a lot. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Leo, many people think chess players have amazing memories. Is memory the most important skill? || Guest: Memory helps, especially for openings, but understanding is more important. If you only remember moves, you get lost when your opponent does something unexpected. A good player asks why a move works. That way, even in a new position, you can find sensible ideas. I still forget things, especially under time pressure, so I try to stay calm rather than panic. || Interviewer: How do you feel after losing an important game? || Guest: Not great at first. I usually want to be alone for a few minutes. Then I look at the game with my coach and try to find the moment where my plan went wrong. That can be painful, but it helps. If you only study wins, you don't improve much. || Interviewer: Do you think chess has helped you at school? || Guest: Yes, especially with exams. It has taught me to slow down, read the question properly and not choose the first answer that looks good. My teachers probably wish I had learned that earlier, but it definitely helps now. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Leo Martin is sixteen and plays chess in national competitions. Leo, how did you first learn the game?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My grandfather taught me when I was seven. At first I only played because it was something we did together on Sunday afternoons. I became serious about it after beating an older student at a school club, which made me think I could improve.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult about playing in your first competitions?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I expected to feel nervous during the games, and I did, but that wasn't the main problem. I found it hard to sit still for such a long time and stay focused until the end. Some games lasted three hours.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you prepare now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I study famous games and practise online, but I don't spend all night doing it. My coach says physical exercise helps concentration, so I run three times a week before school. It sounds strange, but it works.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy about chess?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Winning is nice, obviously, but it isn't everything. I like the moment when a position suddenly makes sense and I see a plan that wasn't clear before. That feels exciting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do your friends understand your hobby?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some do. Others think chess is boring or only for quiet people. They're surprised when I tell them competitions can feel as tense as any sports match, especially when time is running out.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your ambitions?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'd love to play internationally one day, but I'm realistic. For now, I want to help run a chess club for younger children at my school, because learning early helped me a lot.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Leo, many people think chess players have amazing memories. Is memory the most important skill?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Memory helps, especially for openings, but understanding is more important. If you only remember moves, you get lost when your opponent does something unexpected. A good player asks why a move works. That way, even in a new position, you can find sensible ideas. I still forget things, especially under time pressure, so I try to stay calm rather than panic.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you feel after losing an important game?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not great at first. I usually want to be alone for a few minutes. Then I look at the game with my coach and try to find the moment where my plan went wrong. That can be painful, but it helps. If you only study wins, you don't improve much.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you think chess has helped you at school?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, especially with exams. It has taught me to slow down, read the question properly and not choose the first answer that looks good. My teachers probably wish I had learned that earlier, but it definitely helps now.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: What made Leo take chess seriously?

options.C: joining a club with his friends

questions.explanation: Leo says beating an older student at school made him think he could improve.

questions.question: What did Leo find difficult in his first competitions?

options.A: keeping his attention for a long time

questions.explanation: Leo says it was hard to sit still and stay focused until the end of long games.

questions.question: What does Leo do to help his chess playing?
questions.explanation: He says his coach recommends exercise, so he runs three times a week.

questions.question: What does Leo like most about chess?
questions.explanation: Leo says he likes the moment when he sees a plan that was not clear before.

questions.question: What surprises Leo's friends about chess competitions?
questions.explanation: Leo says friends are surprised competitions can feel as tense as sports matches.

questions.question: What does Leo want to do next?
questions.explanation: Leo says he wants to help run a chess club for younger children at his school.
